risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants who accepted the invitation (n = 296) were randomized to Prolonged Exposure therapy (PE [29]–[31]), Cognitive Therapy (CT [32]), a double-blinded SSRI/placebo condition, and a waiting list that was followed by Delayed PE at five months" Comment: The paper explicitly states that participants were randomized, and the broader trial context (embedded randomized controlled trial, ClinicalTrials.gov identifier, double-blinded SSRI/placebo arm) provides sufficient context to suggest a proper random allocation process even though the exact sequence-generation method is not described.

_evidence.method_quote: Participants who accepted the invitation (n = 296) were randomized to Prolonged Exposure therapy (PE [29]–[31]), Cognitive Therapy (CT [32]), a double-blinded SSRI/placebo condition, and a waiting list that was followed by Delayed PE at five months (for full description see [9], [13]).
_evidence.method_summary: Participants were randomized to multiple intervention arms, but the article does not describe how the allocation sequence was generated.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants who accepted the invitation (n = 296) were randomized to Prolonged Exposure therapy (PE [29]–[31]), Cognitive Therapy (CT [32]), a double-blinded SSRI/placebo condition, and a waiting list that was followed by Delayed PE at five months (for full description see [9], [13])." Comment: The article states that participants were randomized, but it does not describe any concealment mechanism (e.g., central allocation, SNOSE, or identical numbered containers) that would prevent recruiters from foreseeing the next assignment. The extracted evidence explicitly notes that the concealment mechanism was not described, so allocation concealment cannot be judged low risk; absence of detail is insufficient to call high risk.

_evidence.concealment_quote: Participants who accepted the invitation (n = 296) were randomized to Prolonged Exposure therapy (PE [29]–[31]), Cognitive Therapy (CT [32]), a double-blinded SSRI/placebo condition, and a waiting list that was followed by Delayed PE at five months (for full description see [9], [13]).
_evidence.note: The article describes randomization and blinding of the SSRI/placebo condition, but gives no details on how the upcoming assignment was concealed from the recruiter.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants who accepted the invitation (n = 296) were randomized to Prolonged Exposure therapy (PE [29]–[31]), Cognitive Therapy (CT [32]), a double-blinded SSRI/placebo condition, and a waiting list that was followed by Delayed PE at five months" and "Telephone- and clinical interviewers were blind to subjects' participation in the embedded steps (i.e., attending clinical interviews for telephone interviewers and attending treatment for clinical interviewers)." Comment: The pharmacologic arm was double-blinded, but the study also included psychotherapy and waiting-list/treatment conditions, and the article only specifies blinding of interviewers—not that participants and the personnel delivering PE/CT were blinded. Because the main outcomes (PTSD symptoms, diagnosis, distress) are subjective and could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of assignment, the dominant risk for performance bias is high.

_evidence.blinding_quote: Participants who accepted the invitation (n = 296) were randomized to Prolonged Exposure therapy (PE [29]–[31]), Cognitive Therapy (CT [32]), a double-blinded SSRI/placebo condition, and a waiting list that was followed by Delayed PE at five months (for full description see [9], [13]). ... Telephone- and clinical interviewers were blind to subjects' participation in the embedded steps (i.e., attending clinical interviews for telephone interviewers and attending treatment for clinical interviewers).
_evidence.blinding_method: double-blinded SSRI/placebo condition; telephone- and clinical interviewers were blind to subjects' participation in the embedded steps

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Telephone- and clinical interviewers were blind to subjects' participation in the embedded steps" and "The PTSD Symptom Scale (PSS) quantified PTSD symptoms at all time-points. The PSS interviewer version (PSS-I)... was used during telephone interviews... The self-administered version of the PSS (PSS-SR)... was used during clinical assessments." Comment: The main outcome assessments (PTSD symptom interviews and related clinical assessments) were conducted by blinded interviewers, which reduces detection bias. Although some measures were self-administered, the study explicitly states interviewer blinding for the assessed interviews, and the outcome measurements are structured symptom scales rather than subjective clinician judgments without blinding.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: Telephone- and clinical interviewers were blind to subjects' participation in the embedded steps (i.e., attending clinical interviews for telephone interviewers and attending treatment for clinical interviewers). The PTSD Symptom Scale (PSS) quantified PTSD symptoms at all time-points. The PSS interviewer version (PSS-I; [35]) was used during telephone interviews... The self-administered version of the PSS (PSS-SR; [36]) was used during clinical assessments.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "We utilized individuals who had data available at ten days and at least two additional time points. Additionally, individuals whose data were collected at inconsistent time intervals from the rest of the sample ... were not included. The final sample for the current study was n = 957" Comment: The analysis was restricted to a derived subset rather than the full randomized cohort, and the article does not report per-arm randomized vs analysed numbers or attrition reasons for the analysed outcome. Although FIML was used, the available text does not let us determine the amount or balance of post-randomization loss for the outcome of interest, so this cannot be judged Low or High from the reported data.

per_arm_flow.reasons: Current study sample was restricted to "individuals who had data available at ten days and at least two additional time points"; final sample included 163 in this class.
Current study sample was restricted to "individuals who had data available at ten days and at least two additional time points"; final sample included 258 in this class.
Current study sample was restricted to "individuals who had data available at ten days and at least two additional time points"; final sample included 536 in this class.

_evidence.notes: Analyses were restricted to a derived subset (n = 957) rather than the full randomized cohort; the paper states "We utilized individuals who had data available at ten days and at least two additional time points" and used "robust full information maximum-likelihood (FIML) procedures." Per-arm randomised counts for the CBT vs no-treatment allocation were not reported for the analysed sample.
